sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 31, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Token at [30,30]-[32,32] blocked by purple wall. UP didn't move it (only HUD changed). Need to go LEFT back to platform, then find path around wall.
- Plan: Move the active right token UP over the purple wall, RIGHT past it, then DOWN into the right container.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 30 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The token moved RIGHT from [27,24]-[29,26] to [27,27]-[29,29]. Let me check if I can continue moving RIGHT. The purple wall is at cols 33-38. The token is now at cols 27-29. If I move RIGHT again, it goes to cols 30-32, then hits the wall.
But wait - I need to reconsider the game. Maybe the goal isn't to put the token in the right container. Maybe the goal is to put it in the LEFT container (which is already occupied by the other token). Or maybe the goal is to get the token to a specific position.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The left container is at [32,11]-[36,15], and the left token is inside at [33,12]-[35,14]. The right container is at [26,44]-[30,48].
What if the goal is to put the right token in the LEFT container? But the left container is already occupied. Unless the tokens can stack or swap.
Or maybe the goal is to put the right token in the right container, but there's a different path. Let me try moving the token UP further to see if there's a gap above the wall.
The wall is at rows 21-41. If I move the token UP from [27,27]-[29,29], it goes to [24,27]-[26,29]. The wall starts at row 21, so the token would still be below the top of the wall. Let me try UP again.
Actually, wait. The token is at [27,27]-[29,29]. If I move UP, it goes to [24,27]-[26,29]. The wall is at rows 21-41, so rows 24-26 are still within the wall's range. But the wall is at cols 33-38, and the token is at cols 27-29. So the token is to the LEFT of the wall, not overlapping with it.
If I move RIGHT from [24,27]-[26,29], the token goes to [24,30]-[26,32]. Then another RIGHT would try to go to [24,33]-[26,35], which overlaps with the wall at cols 33-38. So the wall still blocks.
Hmm, but what if the wall doesn't extend all the way to row 21? Let me check the wall's exact boundaries again.
From the segmentation: id=2, color=p, px=126, [21,33]-[41,38]. The wall is at rows 21-41, cols 33-38. So the wall spans the full height of the platforms.
OK, so the wall blocks all paths from the left platform to the right platform. Unless there's a way to go around the wall from above or below.
The platforms are at rows 21-41. The wall is also at rows 21-41. So the wall spans the full height of the platforms. There's no gap above or below the wall within the platform area.
But what if the token can move outside the platform area? For example, if the token moves UP from the top of the left platform (row 21), it goes to row 18, which is outside the platform. But there's no surface there, so the token can't move.
Wait, but the token DID move UP from [30,24]-[32,26] to [27,24]-[29,26], and then RIGHT to [27,27]-[29,29]. So the token can move on the platform surface. The platform is at rows 21-41, cols 9-29. The token is at [27,27]-[29,29], which is on the platform (rows 27-29 are within 21-41, cols 27-29 are within 9-29).
If I move UP again, the token goes to [24,27]-[26,29], which is still on the platform (rows 24-26 are within 21-41). If I move UP again, the token goes to [21,27]-[23,29], which is at the top of the platform (row 21 is the top). If I move UP again, the token goes to [18,27]-[20,29], which is outside the platform.
Let me try moving UP twice to get to the top of the platform, then RIGHT to see if there's a path.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check token position, then try UP twice to reach top of platform
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W'] and n['pixels'] <= 10:
        b = n['boundary']
        minr = min(p[0] for p in b)
        minc = min(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"id={n['id']:2d} color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']:3d} [{minr},{minc}]")
# Token at [27,27]-[29,29], move UP twice to [21,27]-[23,29]
action(['UP', 'UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
id= 6 color=N px=  8 [30,27]
id= 8 color=W px=  1 [31,28]
id=10 color=N px=  8 [33,12]
